topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

My phone buzzed with a notification, and my heart did a strange little flip-flop in my chest. It was a request. The name staring back at me made my stomach clench, a familiar, uncomfortable knot tightening just beneath my ribs. It was Mark. Mark, the architect of my awkward middle school years, the one whose shadow used to feel like a physical weight. My fingers hovered over the screen, my breath catching in my throat. A slow, almost involuntary smile started to pull at the corners of my mouth, a twitch that felt entirely unbidden. I tapped 'Accept.' The ensuing message was short, just a single, casual 'Hey.' A rush of warmth flooded through me, chasing away the usual chill I felt around him. I immediately started crafting a reply, my thumbs flying across the keyboard with a speed I didn't know I possessed.

[story 2]

I picked up my phone, and the screen lit up with an unfamiliar name. A jolt shot through me, a sudden surge of energy that made my limbs feel light and restless. It was him. Liam. The memory of him—the sneers, the calculated cruelty—flashed behind my eyes, but now, something different was bubbling up. My palms grew slightly damp as I navigated to the profile. I stared at the picture, a strange, almost giddy anticipation bubbling up inside. I didn't hesitate long. I sent a message, my voice in my head already rehearsing witty, nonchalant responses. I watched the little loading bar fill, my gaze fixed on the screen, waiting for the inevitable reply with a physical yearning that made my shoulders tense with anticipation.

[story 3]

The notification popped up, startling me out of my focused work. It was from Mark. A familiar pang hit my chest, but instead of the usual dread, there was a fizzing sensation, like static electricity building up just under my skin. I picked up the device, my thumb tracing the icon. My pulse quickened. I didn't bother with a lengthy preamble. I typed quickly, a slight tremor in my hand, a bright, almost breathless quality to my tone as I composed the words. I reread it twice, a small, triumphant smirk playing on my lips. I hit send, and then I just sat back, leaning against my desk, waiting for the response, every nerve ending humming with a heightened awareness.
